topic: Area of Study 1: Instrumental Music of Haydn, Mozart and Beethoven
subject: music

items.q: What distinguishes Classical (1750-1820) from Baroque style?
items.correct: Clearer melodic phrases, homophonic texture, balanced structures like sonata form.
items.random_wrong: Classical means any old serious music.
items.why: Students use 'classical' loosely. The Classical period specifically reacted against Baroque complexity - simpler textures, clearer cadences, and balanced phrase structures (Mozart, Haydn, early Beethoven).

items.correct: Rapid alternation between the written note and the note a step above.
items.why: Students confuse trill (rapid alternation between two notes) with vibrato (pitch oscillation around one note). Trills add ornamentation; vibrato adds expressive width to a sustained pitch.

items.correct: A cadence ending on chord V, creating a sense of incompleteness.
items.misconception: A cadence that sounds wrong or is performed badly.
items.random_wrong: A cadence using only major chords.
items.why: Students think 'imperfect' means flawed. It's a technical term for an unfinished-sounding cadence - any chord moving to V leaves the music suspended, awaiting resolution.
The answer examiners want is: Schoenberg. “Stravinsky” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which composer is most associated with minimalist works such as 'Music for 18 Musicians'?
items.why: It's Reich. “Glass” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did John Cage compose his silent work '4'33"'?
items.why: Exact answer: 1952. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
